perturbation.original_text: 4. Return of Confidential Information . All Confidential Information and any copies and extracts thereof shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party or at any time within thirty (30) days of receipt of a written request by the disclosing party for the return of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Return of Confidential Information . All Confidential Information and any copies and extracts thereof shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party or at any time within ninety (90) days of receipt of a written request by the disclosing party for the return of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the deadline for returning confidential information to 90 days after a written request could be considered unreasonable and may conflict with trade secret laws that require prompt action to protect such information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA doesn't specify an exact timeframe for returning confidential information, it emphasizes taking reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. A 90-day delay in returning confidential information after a request could be seen as a failure to take such reasonable measures, potentially weakening a claim for trade secret misappropriation under California law. The reasonableness is fact specific, but prompt return is generally expected.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the deadline for returning confidential information to 90 days, potentially conflicting with the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets under CUTSA. The Law Explanation states that a 90-day delay might be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures, weakening a claim for misappropriation. Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'Misappropriation' and 'Improper Means,' highlighting the importance of maintaining secrecy. While the snippet doesn't specify an exact timeframe, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that a prompt return is generally expected, and the changed text's 90-day period could be viewed as unreasonable, thus creating a contradiction under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Arbitration and Equitable Relief. (a) Arbitration . Except as provided herein, the Parties agree that any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance or breach of this Agreement shall be settled by arbitration to be held in San Diego County, California, USA in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules then in effect for the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Arbitration and Equitable Relief. (a) Arbitration . Except as provided herein, the Parties agree that any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance or breach of this Agreement shall be settled by arbitration to be held in San Diego County, California, USA; the arbitrator's decision shall not be subject to judicial review or appeal, even for errors of law.
perturbation.explanation: By preventing judicial review or appeal, including for errors of law, conflicts with the ability of federal courts to correct or vacate arbitration awards under specific circumstances as defined in The Federal Arbitration Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S.C. § 10 allows a court to vacate an arbitration award under certain conditions, such as corruption, fraud, or evident partiality of the arbitrator. Waiving all judicial review, even for errors of law, could prevent a party from challenging an award that falls under these conditions, thus conflicting with the FAA's provisions for judicial oversight of arbitration.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly prevents judicial review or appeal, even for errors of law. This conflicts with 9 U.S.C. § 10, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which allows a U.S. court to vacate an arbitration award if it was 'procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means' or if 'the arbitrators exceeded their powers'. By removing the possibility of judicial review, the altered contract language directly undermines the ability to challenge an arbitration award based on the grounds established in the FAA, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Term . This Agreement shall commence when executed and continue for a period of one (1) year. The Parties agree that from the date of first receipt, and for a period of three (3) years following the last disclosure of Confidential Information, the party receiving the Confidential Information (the “Receiving Party”) shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any other person, or entity, or use for its own benefit except as provided in this Agreement and shall use the same degree of care to avoid publication or dissemination of such information as it does for its own confidential information which it does not desire to have published or disseminated.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Term . This Agreement shall commence when executed and continue for a period of one (1) year. The Parties agree that from the date of first receipt, and for a period of six (6) months following the last disclosure of Confidential Information, the party receiving the Confidential Information (the “Receiving Party”) shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any other person, or entity, or use for its own benefit except as provided in this Agreement and shall use the same degree of care to avoid publication or dissemination of such information as it does for its own confidential information which it does not desire to have published or disseminated.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to six months after the last disclosure of confidential information may be deemed an unreasonably short period to protect trade secrets, conflicting with requirements to take reasonable measures to protect the information. In some cases, this could undermine trade secret protection under state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA does not specify a precise duration for confidentiality, it requires "reasonable efforts" to maintain secrecy (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426). A six-month confidentiality period may be insufficient to protect the value of certain trade secrets, potentially failing the "reasonable efforts" standard required to maintain trade secret status, particularly depending on the nature of the information disclosed. It would depend on the type of confidential information shared and how long it would take a competitor to independently develop such information.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to six months. The Law Explanation, referencing Cal. Civ. Code § 3426, states that the Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. The Perturbation Explanation states that reducing the confidentiality period to six months may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the requirements to take reasonable measures to protect the information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.' Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction because the reduced confidentiality period could be deemed an unreasonable effort to maintain secrecy, violating the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Legal Expenses . If any action or proceeding is brought for enforcement of this Agreement, or because of an alleged or actual dispute, breach, default, or misrepresentation in connection with any of the provisions of this Agreement, the successful or prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorney’s fees and other costs incurred in such action or proceeding in addition to any other relief to which it may be entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Legal Expenses . If any action or proceeding is brought for enforcement of this Agreement, or because of an alleged or actual dispute, breach, default, or misrepresentation in connection with any of the provisions of this Agreement, the successful or prevailing party shall only be entitled to recover attorney's fees if they provide evidence that the other party acted in bad faith.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting attorney's fees recovery to only cases where bad faith is proven could conflict with California statutes that allow for attorney's fees in contract disputes, regardless of bad faith, depending on the contract's specific language and subject matter.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1717 generally provides for reciprocal attorney's fees in contract actions when the contract provides for attorney's fees to one party. While parties can contractually modify this right, restricting fees only to cases of 'bad faith' could be challenged if the original agreement (or the implied understanding under California law) suggests a broader right to attorney's fees for the prevailing party in contract disputes. It creates a higher standard than typically applied under § 1717, potentially conflicting with its provisions depending on the contract's construction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of attorney’s
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code Section 1717. The original text allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, while the modified text restricts this recovery to cases where bad faith is proven. California Civil Code Section 1717(a) states that if a contract provides for attorney's fees to the prevailing party, that party 'shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees'. The modification introduces a condition (proof of bad faith) that is not present in the original agreement or necessarily implied by California law, thus creating a contradiction, because the changed text restricts the prevailing party's right to attorney's fees, while the law states that the prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees.
